key_insight: Reward design does not just change driving performance—it reshapes what a reinforcement learning agent attends to, including path cues, hazards, and collision-risk signals.
engineering_relevance: In autonomous driving systems, attention analysis can serve as a practical diagnostic to verify that reward functions produce the intended safety behavior and representation priorities.
method: Compared three Perceiver-based agents with identical architecture and training data but different reward setups.
Analyzed cross-attention over 50 real-world Waymo Open Motion Dataset scenarios.
Used within-episode correlation with Fisher z aggregation to correctly measure the attention–risk relationship.
evidence_signal: Across 50 real-world scenarios, the validated correlation method showed a robust positive attention–risk link, while reward variants changed attention to GPS tokens by up to 2.0× versus proximity-penalized agents and 4.7× versus no-navigation agents.
watch_item: Naïvely pooling timesteps across episodes can misestimate the attention–risk relationship, so evaluation must preserve within-episode structure.
paper_title: Reward-Conditioned Attention: How Reward Design Shapes What Autonomous Driving Agents See
